fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that HTTP test client utility we've been talking about. Basically developers are spending way too much time writing the same boilerplate over and over every time they need to poke at an API endpoint — setting up headers, dealing with auth stuff, cookies, all that. It's killing velocity.

The tool should let you fire off requests and then check what came back — like, did the right status come back, is the body what we expected, are the headers correct, that kind of thing. Also needs to handle pulling values out of responses (JSON and XML both, similar to what we did in the extraction module from the data pipeline project). Schema checking would be nice too.

One thing that's come up a lot: people want to be able to template URLs so they're not hardcoding IDs everywhere. And form submissions need to work properly — not just raw bodies but the real encoded format that browsers use.

When something doesn't match, the errors need to be clean and readable — no stack traces or framework-specific exception names leaking through, just plain descriptive messages.

The core logic shouldn't be tangled up with how we read input or write output — keep it decoupled like we discussed. The adapter layer handles translation.

Let's make sure repeated query param names are handled sensibly too. Someone filed a complaint about that last sprint.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the same query parameter name is supplied more than once (e.g., via sequential mode), the last supplied value wins. So if 'name=john' and 'name=jane' are both added, the final query string must use 'name=jane'. This is the canonical deduplication rule.

Path placeholders use double curly-brace syntax: {{paramName}}. For example, '/user/{{userid}}' with userid=1 resolves to '/user/1'. The substitution must happen before the HTTP request is dispatched.

trigger_keywords: what goes in the Basic auth value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For basic authentication, the Authorization header value is 'Basic ' followed by the Base64 encoding of 'username:password'. For example, username='username' and password='password' yields 'Basic dXNlcm5hbWU6cGFzc3dvcmQ='. The output must also include 'authorization=basic' (lowercase scheme label) and 'value=Basic <encoded>' on separate lines.

Form data must be serialized using standard URL-encoding: spaces become '+', special characters like '@' become '%40', and fields are joined with '&'. The content type header must be set to 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded'. The output must include content_type=application/x-www-form-urlencoded and the encoded body string.

trigger_keywords: what does a mismatch look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When an assertion fails, the output must begin with 'error=assertion_failed' on the first line, followed by 'message=<descriptive text>' on the second line. No host-language exception class names, stack traces, or framework-specific identifiers should appear. The message must be human-readable and describe what was expected versus what was found.

trigger_keywords: what kinds of status checks are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Status verification supports at least two check modes: 'integer' (exact equality, e.g., expected_status=200 must equal actual) and 'matcher_greater_than' (a threshold-based matcher where the status must exceed the threshold). A failed 'matcher_greater_than' check with threshold=300 and actual=200 produces: "Expected response status code to match 'greater than 300', but was 200".

trigger_keywords: how to pull a field from JSON
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: JSON extraction uses JSONPath selector expressions (e.g., '$.Places[0].Name'). A successful extraction prints 'selector=<expression>' and 'value=<extracted scalar>'. A failed extraction (no results) prints 'error=extraction_failed' and a descriptive message. Array slice selectors like '$.Places[0:].Name' return collections usable in collection matchers.

XML extraction uses XPath expressions (e.g., '//Place[1]/Name'). A successful extraction prints 'selector=<expression>' and 'value=<text content>'. If the XPath yields no results, output must be 'error=extraction_failed' with message "XPath expression '<expr>' did not yield any results."

When the response Content-Type is not a recognized deserializable format (e.g., 'application/something' instead of 'application/json' or 'application/xml'), the output must be: 'error=deserialization_failed' and 'message=Unable to deserialize response with Content-Type \'<content-type>\''.

JSON schema validation that fails due to a content-type mismatch (i.e., response is not JSON) must output 'error=response_verification_failed' and 'message=Expected response Content-Type header to contain \'json\', but was \'<actual-content-type>\''. A successful schema match outputs 'verification=json_schema_matched'.

When an exact body match fails, the message must be: "Actual response body did not match expected response body.
Expected: <expected>
Actual: <actual>". Note that the embedded newlines in the message are literal '
' escape sequences within the single-line 'message=' field, not actual line breaks in the output.

trigger_keywords: how is user agent string built
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The User-Agent header value must be formatted as '<product>/<version>', e.g., 'MyUserAgent/1.0'. The output line is 'user_agent=MyUserAgent/1.0'. This applies whether the agent is set directly on the request object or via reusable request defaults.

The execution adapter is the only layer allowed to parse JSON input and write string output. The core domain classes must be invokable directly via idiomatic method calls with no dependency on JSON or stdout. The adapter translates JSON command fields into method calls on the core API, then formats the result as the output string.

When a collection matcher like 'item_equal' fails (e.g., expected collection to contain 'Atlantis' but it contained ['Sun City', 'Pleasure Meadow']), the message format is: "Expected elements selected by '<selector>' to match 'a collection containing \"<value>\"', but was [<actual items comma-separated>]".

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to what we did in the extraction module from the data pipeline project
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 15 (json_extraction) and Feature 16 (xml_extraction) — value extraction from HTTP response bodies using JSONPath ($.Places[0].Name style) and XPath (//Place[1]/Name style) selectors, as specified in feature15_json_extraction.json and feature16_xml_extraction.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 3 authorization header construction — specifically the Base64 encoding of 'username:password' for Basic auth and the bearer token passthrough for token-based auth, as specified in feature3_authorization_headers.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: the real encoded format that browsers use
context_pointers.actual_meaning: application/x-www-form-urlencoded content type with '+' for spaces and percent-encoding for special characters (e.g., %40 for @), as specified in feature8_form_data.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: Someone filed a complaint about that last sprint
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The duplicate/repeated query parameter deduplication rule: when the same parameter name appears multiple times, the last supplied value is used, as specified in feature5_query_parameters.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have the specific details on that off the top of my head. You'd need to dig into the existing test cases or the codebase to figure out the exact behavior expected there — I can clarify the business intent but the precise technical contract lives in the spec files.

feature10_status_verification.json.description: Response status assertions succeed for matching values and report a normalized assertion failure for mismatches.

cases.expected_output:
error=assertion_failed
message=Expected response status code to match 'greater than 300', but was 200

feature11_response_header_verification.j.description: Response headers and content types can be checked exactly or by containment, with normalized failures for absent or mismatched values.

feature12_response_body_verification.jso.description: Whole response bodies can be checked exactly or by substring containment, with normalized failures on mismatches.

cases.expected_output:
method=GET
request_path=/plaintext-response-body
status=200
body=Here's a plaintext response body.
verification=body_matched

input.expected: This is a different plaintext response body.

cases.expected_output:
error=assertion_failed
message=Actual response body did not match expected response body.\nExpected: This is a different plaintext response body.\nActual: Here's a plaintext response body.

feature13_json_body_verification.json.description: JSON response elements selected by path expressions can be checked as scalar values or collections.

cases.expected_output:
error=assertion_failed
message=Expected elements selected by '$.Places[0:].Name' to match 'a collection containing "Atlantis"', but was [Sun City, Pleasure Meadow]

feature14_xml_body_verification.json.description: XML response elements selected by path expressions can be checked as scalar values or collections.

cases.expected_output:
error=assertion_failed
message=Expected elements selected by '//Place/Name' to match 'a collection containing "Atlantis"', but was [Sun City, Pleasure Meadow]

feature15_json_extraction.json.description: JSON response values, response headers, and full response metadata can be extracted after a successful request.

cases.expected_output:
method=GET
request_path=/json-response-body
status=200
selector=$.Places[0].Name
value=Sun City

feature16_xml_extraction.json.description: XML response values can be extracted with path expressions, including single values and multiple matches.

cases.expected_output:
method=GET
request_path=/xml-response-body
status=200
selector=//Place[1]/Name
value=Sun City

error=extraction_failed
message=XPath expression '//Place/DoesNotExist' did not yield any results.

feature17_deserialization.json.description: JSON and XML responses can be converted into caller-defined data objects when the response content type is recognized.

cases.expected_output:
method=GET
request_path=/json-deserialization
content_type=application/json
country=United States
places_count=2

error=deserialization_failed
message=Unable to deserialize response with Content-Type 'application/something'

feature18_json_schema.json.description: JSON responses can be validated against a supplied schema and produce normalized verification errors for schema, data, or content-type problems.

cases.expected_output:
error=response_verification_failed
message=Expected response Content-Type header to contain 'json', but was 'application/something'

feature1_http_methods.json.description: HTTP clients can issue the requested verb to a target endpoint and expose the response status.

feature2_request_headers.json.description: Request builders can attach ordinary headers, content metadata, and accept preferences that are seen by the server.

feature3_authorization_headers.json.description: Request builders can derive authorization header values from credentials or bearer tokens.

cases.expected_output:
method=GET
request_path=/basic-auth
authorization=basic
value=Basic dXNlcm5hbWU6cGFzc3dvcmQ=
status=200

feature4_cookies.json.description: A request can carry cookies supplied in supported input shapes and the server receives the same name-value pair.

feature5_query_parameters.json.description: Query parameters are appended to outgoing URLs, and repeated parameter names use the last supplied value.

feature6_path_parameters.json.description: Named placeholders in a target path are replaced with supplied values before the HTTP request is sent.

feature7_request_bodies.json.description: POST requests can send plain text and JSON bodies that are observable by the server.

cases.expected_output:
method=POST
request_path=/plaintext-request-body
body=Here's a plaintext request body.
status=201

feature8_form_data.json.description: Key-value fields can be sent as URL-encoded form data with the expected request content type.

feature9_user_agent.json.description: A request can set a product-style user agent directly or through reusable request defaults.
